subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the role of the individual in classroom and school participation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in activities that focus on a classroom or school issue or problem.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate respect for the rights of others in discussions, regardless of whether one agrees with the other viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how people earn money and explain other ways that people receive money.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the concepts of geography, economics, and history that apply to his/her family.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe an event in his/her family.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify similarities and/or differences between him/her and others with detail.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify similarities and differences between neighborhoods.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and identify patterns of continuity in his/her family.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify events of the past, present, and future in his/her family life.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify causes and effects, using examples from his/her family life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the concept of time measurements, including days, weeks, months and years.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Create understanding of the past by using primary and secondary sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify opinions of others.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize different forms of evidence used to make meaning in social studies (including sources such as art and photographs, artifacts, oral histories, maps, and graphs).

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop questions about his/her family.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore how communities share resources and services with other communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: At times, neighboring communities share resources and workers to support multiple communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explain the services provided by community workers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will identify different types of jobs performed in their community.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will distinguish between goods and services and identify goods produced in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Goods are the products a person or group of people makes. Services are actions performed by a person or group of people with a certain skill.

subject: Social Studies
description: A community requires the interdependence of many people performing a variety of jobs and services to provide basic needs and wants.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore the purpose of taxes and how they are collected in their communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Taxes are collected to provide communities with goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how consumers react to changes in the prices of goods.

subject: Social Studies
description: Scarcity, the price of goods and services, and choice all influence economic decisions made by individuals and communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore economic decision making and the use of money.

subject: Social Studies
description: People make decisions to buy, sell, and use money based on their needs, wants, and the availability of resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how available resources differ in communities (e.g., home-grown food available in rural farm areas vs. shopping in supermarkets).

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate what resources are available in their community and what resources are obtained from neighboring communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: The availability of resources to meet basic needs varies across urban, suburban, and rural communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communities face different challenges in meeting their needs and wants.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will distinguish between cause and effect and will examine changes in their community in terms of cause and effect (e.g., automobiles and the growth of suburbs, growing population in suburban areas, and reduction of farms).

subject: Social Studies
description: Cause-and-effect relationships help us to understand the changes in communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Cause-and-effect relationships help us recount events and understand historical development.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will develop a time line for their community, including important events, such as when the school was built.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine continuities and changes over time in their community, using evidence such as maps, population charts, photographs, newspapers, biographies, artifacts, and other historical materials.

subject: Social Studies
description: Continuities and changes over time in communities can be examined by interpreting evidence such as maps, population charts, photographs, newspapers, biographies, artifacts, and other historical materials.

subject: Social Studies
description: Continuities and changes over time in communities can be described using historical thinking, vocabulary, and tools such as time lines.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identifying continuities and changes over time can help understand historical developments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will use maps and legends to identify major physical features, such as mountains, rivers, lakes, and oceans of the local community, New York State, and the nation.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will use a compass rose to identify cardinal (North, South, East, West) and intermediate (Northeast, Southeast, Southwest, Northwest) directions on maps and in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: The location and place of physical features and man-made structures can be described using symbols and specific geography vocabulary.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will describe the means people create for moving people, goods, and ideas in their communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore how humans have positively and negatively affected the environment of their community though such features as roads, highways, buildings, bridges, shopping malls, railroads, and parks.

subject: Social Studies
description: Humans modify the environment of their communities through housing, transportation systems, schools, marketplaces, and recreation areas.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: The location of physical features and natural resources often affects where people settle and may affect how those people sustain themselves.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will create maps including maps that represent their classroom, school, or community, and maps that illustrate places in stories.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will locate their communities on maps and/or globes.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Geography and natural resources shape where and how urban, suburban, and rural communities develop and how they sustain themselves.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Citizens provide service to their community in a variety of ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will identify who makes and enforces the rules and laws in their community. They will also explore how leaders make and enforce these rules and laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communities have leaders who are responsible for making laws and enforcing laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explain the importance of making fair laws and rules, the benefits of following them, and the consequences of violating them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communities have the responsibility to make and enforce fair laws and rules that provide for the common good.

subject: Social Studies
description: Communities have rules and laws that affect how they function. Citizens contribute to a community's government through leadership and service.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Geographic factors often influence where people settle and form communities. People adapt to and modify their environment in different ways to meet their needs.

subject: Social Studies

Students will locate each selected world community in relationship to principal parallels (equator, Tropic of Cancer, Tropic of Capricorn, Arctic Circle, and Antarctic Circle) and meridians (Prime Meridian) using cardinal and intermediate directions.

subject: Social Studies
description: World communities can be located in relation to each other and to principle parallels and meridians.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine where each selected world community is located.

subject: Social Studies
description: World communities can be located on globes and maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: The location of world communities can be described using geographic tools and vocabulary.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine a variety of maps for at least two of the selected world communities, looking for structural features of the map such as title, legend or key, compass orientation, author, date, grid, and scale. These should include political, physical, vegetation, and resource maps. A variety of scale should be represented (e.g., continent vs. country, country vs. city).

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will identify the differences between a globe and a map.

subject: Social Studies
description: Globes, maps, photographs, and satellite images contain geographic information. Maps often have a title, legend or key, compass orientation, author, date, grid, and scale.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will locate the selected world communities in relation to oceans and continents.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will identify the continents and oceans, by using globes and maps.

subject: Social Studies
description: Earth is comprised of water and large land masses that can be divided into distinct regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geographic regions have unifying characteristics and can be studied using a variety of tools.

description: Identify rights and responsibilities of citizens in the local community and compare them to those in world communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify leaders of world communities and the president of the United States; identify similarities and differences in their roles.

description: Identify situations in which social actions are required and suggest actions.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in activities that focus on a classroom, school, or world community issue or problem.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate respect for the rights of others in discussions and classroom debates, regardless of whether one agrees with the other viewpoints.

description: Explore the types of governments in world communities and the services that they provide to citizens.

subject: Social Studies

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify products found in world communities and the various ways that people in those communities pay for products.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the variety of resources available in a particular world community used to produce goods and/or provide services.

description: Examine how scarcity affects the decisions about the use of resources by people and governments; examine the costs and benefits of economic decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize the relationship between geography, economics, and history in world communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe a historical event in a world community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a world region by describing a characteristic that places within it have in common.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and identify patterns of continuity and change in world communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize periods of time, such as decades and centuries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Immigration And Migration From The Early 1800s To The Present: Many people have immigrated and migrated to New York State contributing to its cultural growth and development.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate major economic activities in regions of New York State and create a map showing the major economic activities in Long Island, New York City, Lower Hudson Valley, Mid- Hudson Valley, Capital District, Adirondacks/North Country, Mohawk Valley/Central New York, Mid-West/Finger Lakes, Catskills, Southern Tier, and Western New York.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how the economic activities in their local community have changed over the last 50 years.

subject: Social Studies
description: As manufacturing moved out of New York State, service industries and high-technology industries have grown.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will trace manufacturing and industrial development in New York State and in their local community in terms of what major products were produced, who produced them, and for whom they were produced from the 1800s to today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Between 1865 and 1915, rapid industrialization occurred in New York State. Over time, industries and manufacturing continued to grow.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will research several people who made important contributions to business, technology, and New York State communities. Some people to consider include Thomas Jennings, Thomas Edison, Henry Steinway, John Jacob Bausch, Henry Lomb, Cornelius Vanderbilt, Lewis H. Latimer, Jacob Schoellkopf, Nikola Tesla, George Westinghouse, George Eastman, Amory Houghton, Willis Carrier, John D. Rockefeller, Edward H. Harriman, J.P. Morgan, Hetty Green, Emily Roebling, and Elisha Otis, and others, as locally appropriate.

subject: Social Studies
description: Entrepreneurs and inventors associated with New York State have made important contributions to business and technology.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the importance of New York City in the development of banking and finance in New York State and the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore which resources were extracted in New York State over time, the location of those resources, and the economic activities associated with those resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine New York State's key agricultural products during the 1800s and compare these to the key agricultural products of today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Farming, mining, lumbering, and finance are important economic activities associated with New York State.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will trace developments in transportation and communication technology from the 1800s to the present, noting the effects that these changes had on their communities, the State, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate which early means of transportation were used in their local community and to which communities they were linked, noting why they were linked to those communities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Improved technology such, as the steam engine and the telegraph made transportation and communication faster and easier. Later developments in transportation and communication technology had an effect on communities, the State, and the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will locate and name at least five towns and four cities along the canal, and identify major products shipped using the canal.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how the development of the canal affected the Haudenosaunee nations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the physical features of New York State and determine where it might be easiest to build a canal, and form a hypothesis about the best location. Students will compare their hypothesis with the actual location of the Erie Canal.

subject: Social Studies
description: In order to connect the Great Lakes with the Atlantic Ocean, the Erie Canal was built. Existing towns expanded and new towns grew along the canal. New York City became the busiest port in the country.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the difficulties of traveling west at this time and methods used to improve travel on roads, including corduroy roads and turnpikes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine why people began to move west in New York State.

subject: Social Studies
description: After the Revolution, New Yorkers began to move and settle farther west, using roads many of which had begun as Native American trails.

subject: Social Studies
description: Westward Movement And Industrialization: New York State played an important role in the growth of the United States. During the 1800s, people traveled west looking for opportunities. Economic activities in New York State are varied and have changed over time, with improvements in transportation and technology.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will research a local community's contribution to the Civil War effort, using resources such as war memorials, a local library, reenactments, historical associations, and museum artifacts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore how New York State supported the Union during the Civil War; providing soldiers, equipment, and food.

subject: Social Studies
description: The United States became divided over several issues, including slavery, resulting in the Civil War. New York State supported the Union and played an important role in this war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate people who took action to bring about change, such as Amelia Bloomer, Sojourner Truth, Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Lucretia Mott, Susan B. Anthony, Matilda Joslyn Gage, and Elizabeth Blackwell. Students will explore what happened at the convention of women in Seneca Falls.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the rights denied to women during the 1800s.

subject: Social Studies
description: Women have not always had the same rights as men in the United States and New York State. They sought to expand their rights and bring about change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate people who took action to abolish slavery, including Samuel Cornish, Fredrick Douglass, William Lloyd Garrison, and Harriet Tubman.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine life as a slave in New York State.

subject: Social Studies
description: There were slaves in New York State. People worked to fight against slavery and for change.

subject: Social Studies
description: In Search Of Freedom And A Call For Change: Different groups of people did not have equal rights and freedoms. People worked to bring about change. The struggle for rights and freedoms was one factor in the division of the United States that resulted in the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will discuss active citizenship and adults' responsibility to vote, to understand important issues, and to serve on a jury.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will learn their responsibilities as citizens, such as obeying rules and laws (e.g., traffic safety, see something–say something, anti-bullying).

subject: Social Studies
description: Citizens of the State of New York have responsibilities that help their nation, their state, and their local communities function. Some responsibilities are stated in laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the rights and freedoms guaranteed to citizens.

subject: Social Studies
description: New Yorkers have rights and freedoms that are guaranteed in the United States Constitution, in the New York State Constitution, and by state laws.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the structure of their local government and its relationship to state government. Students will be able to identify the elected leaders of their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will identify the borough of New York City in which they live, and in which county the borough is located.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will identify the county in which they live, noting where their city, town or village is within that county.

subject: Social Studies
description: Government in New York State is organized into counties, cities, towns, and villages.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate the steps necessary for a bill to become a law in New York State.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will use a graphic organizer to show the different branches of state government and the roles and responsibilities of each. The present governor, the local senator, and the local assemblyperson should be identified.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the elements of the New York State Seal adopted in 1777 and the New York State flag and explain the symbols used.

subject: Social Studies
description: The New York State Constitution establishes the basic structure of government for the state. The government of New York creates laws to protect the people and interests of the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore ways that the federal, state, and local governments meet the needs of citizens, looking for similarities and differences between the different levels of government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the basic structure of the federal government, including the president, Congress, and the courts.

subject: Social Studies
description: After the Revolution, the United States of America established a federal government; colonies established state governments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Government: There are different levels of government within the United States and New York State. The purpose of government is to protect the rights of citizens and to promote the common good. The government of New York State establishes rights, freedoms, and responsibilities for its citizens.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate the strategically important battles of Long Island and Saratoga and why the Battle of Saratoga is considered by many to be a turning point. A turning point can be an event in history that brought about significant change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore why African Americans volunteered to fight with the British during the war.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine which Native Americans supported the British and which Native Americans supported the Patriot cause.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine New York's geographic location relative to the other colonies, locate centers of Loyalist support, and examine the extent of the British occupation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine issues of political and economic rights that led to the American Revolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: Growing conflicts between England and the 13 colonies over issues of political and economic rights led to the American Revolution. New York played a significant role during the Revolution, in part due to its geographic location.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the alliances between Native Americans and the English and between Native Americans and the French.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will locate some of the major battles fought in New York State during the French and Indian War, noting why they were important.

subject: Social Studies
description: In the mid-1700s, England and France competed against each other for control of the land and wealth in North America. The English, French, and their Native American allies fought the French and Indian War. Several major battles were fought in New York.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the colonial experience of African Americans, comparing and contrasting life under the Dutch and under the British.

subject: Social Studies
description: Student will investigate colonial life under the Dutch and the English, examining the diverse origins of the people living in the colony.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will trace colonial history from the Dutch colony of New Netherland to the English colony of New York, making note of lasting Dutch contributions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Colonial New York became home to many different peoples, including European immigrants, and free and enslaved Africans. Colonists developed different lifestyles.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate the interactions and relationships between Native American groups, Dutch and French fur traders, French missionaries, and early settlers, noting the different perspectives toward land ownership and use of resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will map the voyages of Verrazano, Hudson, and Champlain and will determine which Native American peoples encountered these explorers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Europeans in search of a route to Asia explored New York's waterways. Early settlements began as trading posts or missions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Colonial And Revolutionary Period In New York: European exploration led to the colonization of the region that became New York State. Beginning in the early 1600s, colonial New York was home to people from many different countries. Colonial New York was important during the Revolutionary Period.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine contributions of Native Americans that are evident today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine Native American traditions; work specialization and the roles of men, women, and children in their society; transportation systems; and technology.

subject: Social Studies
description: Each Native American group developed a unique way of life with a shared set of customs, beliefs, and values.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will compare and contrast the patterns of organization and governance of Native American groups such as the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) and Munsees, including matrilineal clan structure, decision-making processes, and record keeping, with a focus on local Native American groups.

subject: Social Studies
description: Native American groups developed specific patterns of organization and governance to manage their societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate how Native Americans such as the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) and the Algonquian-speaking peoples adapted to and modified their environment to meet their needs and wants.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the locations of early Native American groups in relation to geographic features, noting how certain physical features are more likely to support settlement and larger populations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geographic factors often influenced locations of early settlements. People made use of the resources and the lands around them to meet their basic needs of food, clothing, and shelter.

subject: Social Studies
description: Native American Groups And The Environment: Native American groups, chiefly the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) and Algonquian-speaking groups, inhabited the region that became New York State. These people interacted with the environment and developed unique cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will use maps of a variety of scales including a map of the United States and the world to identify and locate the country and states that border New York State.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the location of the capital of New York State and the major cities of New York State in relation to their home community, using directionality, and latitude and longitude coordinates.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will create a map of the political features of New York State that includes the capital city and the five most populous cities, as well as their own community.

subject: Social Studies
description: New York State can be represented using a political map that shows cities, capitals, and boundaries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine New York State climate and vegetation maps in relation to a New York State physical map, exploring the relationship between physical features and vegetation grown, and between physical features and climate.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will be able to identify and map New York State's major physical features, including mountains, plateaus, rivers, lakes, and large bodies of water, such as the Atlantic Ocean and Long Island Sound.

subject: Social Studies
description: Physical and thematic maps can be used to explore New York State's diverse geography.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geography Of New York State: New York State has a diverse geography. Various maps can be used to represent and examine the geography of New York State.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify rights and responsibilities as a citizen of the community and the state.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify people in positions of power and how they can influence people's rights and freedom.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify situations in which social actions are required and suggest actions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Show respect in issues involving difference and conflict; participate in negotiating and compromising in the resolution of differences and conflict.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify opportunities for and the role of the individual in social and political participation in the school, local, and/or state community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different types of political systems used at various times in New York State history and, where appropriate, in United States history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in activities that focus on a classroom, school, community, state, or national issue or problem.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate respect for the rights of others in discussions and classroom debates, regardless of whether one agrees with the other viewpoints.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the ways that the government pays for the goods and services that it provides, including tax revenue.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the meaning of unemployment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain why individuals and businesses specialize and trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the role of money in making exchange easier; examine the role of corporations and labor unions in an economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between the various types of resources (human capital, physical capital, and natural resources) required to produce goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity necessitates decision making; compare the costs and benefits of individual and economic decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how human activities alter places and regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize relationships between patterns and processes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify how environments affect human activities and how human activities affect physical environments.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish human activities and human-made features from "environments" (natural events or physical features—land, air, and water—that are not directly made by humans).

subject: Social Studies
description: Use location terms and geographic representations, such as maps, photographs, satellite images, and models, to describe where places are in relation to each other, to describe connections between places, and to evaluate the benefits of particular places for purposeful activities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe historical developments in New York State with specific details, including time and place.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize the relationship between geography, economics, and history in social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare New York State historical events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify multiple perspectives from a historical event.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a region in New York State by describing a characteristic that places within it have in common, and then compare it to other regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and identify patterns of continuity and change in New York State.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use periods of time, such as decades and centuries to put events into chronological order.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize dynamics of historical continuity and change over periods of time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between long-term and immediate causes and effects of an event from his/her life or current events or history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the relationship between multiple causes and multiple effects, using examples from his/her life and from a current event or history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Employ mathematical skills to measure time in years and centuries. Understand the difference between B.C.E. and C.E. Identify the chronological significance of data presented in time lines, with teacher support.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how events are related chronologically to one another.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create understanding of the past by using and analyzing primary and secondary sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify inferences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify arguments of others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and explain creation and /or authorship, purpose, and format of evidence. Where appropriate identify point of view and bias.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize, use, and analyze different forms of evidence to make meaning in social studies (including primary and secondary sources, such as art and photographs, artifacts, oral histories, maps, and graphs).

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop questions about New York State and its history, geography, economics, and/or government.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine products that are exported from the United States to other markets in the Western Hemisphere, noting how this affects the United States economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine products that are imported into markets within the United States based on demand for these products, noting how this affects the United States economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Countries trade with other countries to meet economic needs and wants. They are interdependent.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine why certain products are manufactured in particular places, taking into account the weight, transportation availability, and costs and markets (e.g., soda pop).

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will identify the major natural resources of the United States, Canada, Mexico, and one Caribbean or one South American country to determine the major industries of those countries in relation to available resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Peoples of the Western Hemisphere have engaged in a variety of economic activities to meet their needs and wants.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore the characteristics of a traditional economy used by the Inuit, the market economy of the United States or Canada, and the command economy of Cuba, noting similarities and differences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Different types of economic systems have developed across time and place within the Western Hemisphere. These economic systems, including traditional, market, and command, address the three economic questions: what will be produced, how it will be produced, and who will get what is produced?

subject: Social Studies
description: Economics: The peoples of the Western Hemisphere have developed various ways to meet their needs and wants. Many of the countries of the Western Hemisphere trade with each other, as well as with other countries around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine multinational organizations and nongovernmental organizations and their roles in promoting cooperation, peace, and cultural understanding.

subject: Social Studies
description: Multinational organizations and nongovernmental organizations in the Western Hemisphere seek to encourage cooperation between nations, protect human rights, support economic development, and provide assistance in challenging situations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine at least one group of people, such as Native Americans, African Americans, women, or another cultural, ethnic, or racial minority in the Western Hemisphere, who have struggled or are struggling for equality and civil rights or sovereignty.

subject: Social Studies
description: Across time and place, different groups of people in the Western Hemisphere have struggled and fought for equality and civil rights or sovereignty.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the Declaration of Independence, the United States Constitution and Bill of Rights, the British North America Act, and the Canadian Bill of Rights in terms of key values, beliefs, and principles of constitutional democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Legal, political, and historic documents define the values, beliefs, and principles of constitutional democracy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will compare and contrast the government structures and functions of the United States government with those of Canada, Mexico, and one other country in either the Caribbean or South America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the foundational documents of the United States government for evidence of the country's beliefs, values, and principles.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the basic structure of the United States federal government, including the president, Congress, and the courts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Government structures, functions, and founding documents vary from place to place in the countries of the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Government: The political systems of the Western Hemisphere vary in structure and organization across time and place.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate a current issue that two or more Western Hemisphere countries are facing together. Some examples include environmental issues, immigration, and trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Countries in the Western Hemisphere face a variety of concerns and issues specific to the region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will compare and contrast key cultural characteristics and contributions associated with the United States with those associated with Canada, Mexico, and a country in either the Caribbean or South America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore key cultural characteristics, such as the languages, religions and contributions, of the United States, Canada, Mexico, and one Caribbean or one South American country.

subject: Social Studies
description: The countries of the Western Hemisphere have varied characteristics and contributions that distinguish them from other countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Comparative Cultures: The countries of the Western Hemisphere are diverse and the cultures of these countries are rich and varied. Due to their proximity to each other, the countries of the Western Hemisphere share some of the same concerns and issues.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will use physical, climate, and vegetation maps in combination with population density, land use, and resource distribution maps to discern patterns in human settlement and types of economic activity.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will create a political map of the Western Hemisphere, noting which countries are in which region, and a political map of the United States showing the location of the states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will map the regions within the Western Hemisphere and locate major physical features within each region.

subject: Social Studies
description: The physical environment influences human population distribution, land use, and other forms of economic activity.

subject: Social Studies
description: The Western Hemisphere can be divided into regions. Regions are areas that share common, identifiable characteristics such as physical, political, economic, or cultural features. Regions within the Western Hemisphere include:North America (Canada and the United States), Mesoamerica (Mexico and Central America), Caribbean, and South America

subject: Social Studies
description: Physical maps reflect the varied climate zones, landforms, bodies of water, and natural resources of the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geography In The Western Hemisphere: The diverse geography of the Western Hemisphere has influenced human culture and settlement in distinct ways. Human communities in the Western Hemisphere have modified the physical environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the conditions experienced by enslaved Africans during the Middle Passage.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate why sugar was brought to the Americas, noting where it was grown and why, and the role of supply and demand.

subject: Social Studies
description: Africans were captured, brought to the Americas, and sold as slaves. Their transport across the Atlantic was known as the Middle Passage.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the effect of diseases introduced to the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will map the movements of people, plants, animals, and disease between Europe, the Americas, and Africa.

subject: Social Studies
description: The transatlantic trade of goods, movement of people, and spread of ideas and diseases resulted in cultural diffusion. This cultural diffusion became known as the Columbian Exchange which reshaped the lives and influenced the beliefs of people.

subject: Social Studies
description: French in Canada and the fur trade

subject: Social Studies
description: Conquests by Cortez and Pizarro and the resulting demographic change

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine European interactions with Native Americans, using these examples:

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how Native Americans viewed the newcomers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Europeans encountered and interacted with Native Americans in a variety of ways.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will map the key areas of the Western Hemisphere that were colonized by the English, Dutch, French, Portuguese, and Spanish, comparing the locations, relative sizes, and key resources of these regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate explorers from different European countries and map the areas of the Western Hemisphere where they explored, including Christopher Columbus, John Cabot, Jacques Cartier, Pedro Cabral, and Vasco Nunez de Balboa.

subject: Social Studies
description: Europeans traveled to the Americas in search of new trade routes, including a northwest passage, and resources. They hoped to gain wealth, power, and glory.

subject: Social Studies
description: European Exploration And Its Effects: Various European powers explored and eventually colonized the Western Hemisphere. This had a profound effect on Native Americans and led to the transatlantic slave trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will compare and contrast political states of the Maya and the Aztec, noting the territories that they controlled, the type of rule each had, and how the ruler attempted to unify the people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Political states can take different forms, such as city-states and empires. A city-state is comprised of a city with a government that controls the surrounding territory, while an empire is a political organization developed when a single, supreme authority conquers other geographic and/or cultural regions beyond its initial settlements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will compare how the Mayas, Aztecs, and Incas adapted to and modified their environment to meet the needs of the people, examining the clothing, farming, shelter, and transportation systems for each.

subject: Social Studies
description: Complex societies and civilizations adapted to and modified their environment to meet the needs of their people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will investigate the characteristics of the Mayas, Aztecs, and Incas, noting similarities and differences.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will locate the complex societies and civilizations of the Mayas, Aztecs, and Incas on a map, and students will determine when these societies and civilizations occurred.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civilizations share certain common characteristics of religion, job specialization, cities, government, language and writing systems, technology, and social hierarchy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Complex Societies And Civilizations: Between 1100 B.C.E. and 1500 C.E, complex societies and civilizations developed in the Western Hemisphere. Although these complex societies and civilizations have certain defining characteristics in common, each is also known for unique cultural achievements and contributions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will select one Native American culture group from the United States, one from Canada, and one from the Caribbean region and compare and contrast them by examining how each of these groups adapted to and used the environment and its resources to meet their basic needs, and by examining elements of their culture, including customs, beliefs, values, languages, and patterns of organization and governance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine maps that show the variety of different Native American∗ groups located in the Western Hemisphere, noting that there are many different culture groups in many different types of physical, climate, and vegetative regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Early peoples living together in settlements developed shared cultures with customs, beliefs, values, and languages that give identity to the group. These early peoples also developed patterns of organization and governance to manage their societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human populations that settled along rivers, in rainforests, along oceans, in deserts, on plains, in mountains, and in cold climates adapted to and made use of the resources and environment around them in developing distinct ways of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the various theories of the migration routes by which the first humans may have arrived, including the Bering land bridge, using maps and archaeological evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Various forms of scientific evidence suggest that humans came to North America approximately 25,000 to 14,000 years ago and spread southward to South America.

subject: Social Studies
description: Early Peoples Of The Americas: The first humans in the Western Hemisphere modified their physical environment as well as adapted to their environment. Their interactions with their environment led to various innovations and to the development of unique cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop an understanding of the interdependence of individuals and groups in communities in the Western Hemisphere

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify rights and responsibilities of citizens within societies in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the roles of people in power in the Western Hemisphere, both historically and currently. Identify ways that current figures can influence people’s rights and freedom.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify situations with a global focus in which social actions are required and suggest solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in negotiating and compromising in the resolution of differences and conflict.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify opportunities for and the role of the individual in social and political participation at various times and in various locations in the Western Hemisphere outside the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify different types of political systems and ideologies used at various times and in various locations in the Western Hemisphere, and identify the roles of individuals and key groups in those political and social systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Participate in activities that focus on a localized issue or problem in a country other than the United States in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate respect for the rights of others in discussion and classroom debates, regardless of whether one agrees with the other viewpoint. Consider alternate views in discussion, with teacher support.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe government decisions that affect economies in case studies from the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the meaning of unemployment, inflation, income, and economic growth in the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the role of job specialization and trade historically and during contemporary times in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Provide examples of how currency makes exchange easier by comparing a barter economy to a currency-based economy; examine why corporations and labor unions have a role in a market economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Show examples of various types of resources (human capital, physical capital, and natural resources) required to provide goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity necessitates decision making; employ examples from the Western Hemisphere to illustrate the role of scarcity historically and in current events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how human activities alter places and regions in the Western Hemisphere. 6. Recognize that boundaries and definition of location are historically constructed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and explain how characteristics (cultural, economic, and physical-environmental) of regions affect the history of societies in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe how environments affect human activities and how human activities affect physical environments through the study of cases in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish human activities and human-made features from “environments” (natural events or physical features—land, air, and water—that are not directly made by humans) in the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use location terms and geographic representations such as maps, photographs, satellite images, and models to describe where places in the Western Hemisphere are in relation to each other, to describe connections among places, and to evaluate the benefits of particular places for purposeful activities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe historical developments in the history of the Western Hemisphere with specific references to circumstances of time and place and to connections to broader regional or global processes, with teacher support.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify how the relationship between geography, economics, and history helps to define a context for events in the study of the Western Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare events in the history of the Western Hemisphere in societies in similar chronological contexts and in various geographical contexts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Categorize divergent perspectives of an individual historical event.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a region in the Western Hemisphere by describing a characteristic that places within it have in common, and then compare it to other regions. Understand how regions can be defined as sharing common characteristics in contrast with other regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the role of periodization as a practice in history and social studies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and identify patterns of continuity and change in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use periods of time such as decades and centuries to organize a historical narrative; compare histories in different places in the Western Hemisphere utilizing timelines.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize the dynamics of historical continuity and change over periods of time. Identify important turning points in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between long-term and immediate causes and effects of an event from current events or history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and classify the relationship between multiple causes and multiple effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify causes and effects using examples from current events or grade-level content and historical events

subject: Social Studies
description: Employ mathematical skills to measure time in years and centuries. Understand the difference between B.C.E. and C.E. Identify the chronological significance of data presented in time lines.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how events are related chronologically to one another in time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize arguments on specific social studies topics and identify evidence supporting the argument.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify implicit ideas to draw inference, with support.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify arguments of others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify evidence and explain content, authorship, purpose, and format; identify bias; explain the role of bias and potential audience, with teacher support.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and effectively select different forms of evidence used to make meaning in social studies (including primary and secondary sources such as art and photographs, artifacts, oral histories, maps, and graphs).

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop questions to help identify evidence about topics related to the historical events occurring in the Western Hemisphere that can be answered by gathering, interpreting, and using evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how various technologies affected trade and exchanges. Some examples are types of ships, including junks and caravels; improvements to ships, such as sails and rudders; navigation tools, such as the compass and astrolabe; and gunpowder.

subject: Social Studies
description: Complex societies and civilizations adapted and designed technologies for transportation that allowed them to cross challenging landscapes and move people and goods efficiently.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the spread of the Black Death (Bubonic Plague) as a result of interregional exchange and its effects on various regions within Afro-Eurasia, using a variety of sources, such as maps, poetry, and other primary source documents.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the methods used by the Mongols to enable them to rule over a diverse population, noting how Mongol rule expanded trade.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will map the extent of the Mongol Empire at the height of its power.

subject: Social Studies
description: The Mongol conquests in Eurasia fostered connections between the East and the West, and the Mongols served as important agents of change and cultural diffusion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will study interregional travelers such as Marco Polo, Ibn Battuta, Mansa Musa, and Zheng He and examine why they traveled, the places visited, what was learned, and what was exchanged as a result of their travel.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how the location of resources helped determine the location of trade routes and the economic impact of the exchange of resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will create maps that illustrate items exchanged and ideas spread along the Silk Roads, across the Indian Ocean, and on the Trans-Saharan trade routes.

subject: Social Studies
description: The Silk Roads, the Indian Ocean, and the Trans-Saharan routes formed the major Afro-Eurasian trade networks connecting the East and the West. Ideas, people, technologies, products, and diseases moved along these routes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interactions Across The Eastern Hemisphere (ca. 600 C.E. – ca. 1450): Trade networks promoted the exchange and diffusion of language, belief systems, tools, intellectual ideas, inventions, and diseases.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the conflict of the Crusades from three different perspectives: feudal Europe, Byzantine, and Islamic.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the three distinct cultural regions of the Mediterranean world in terms of their location, the extent of each region at the height of its power, and the political, economic, and social interactions between these regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Competition and rivalry over religious, economic, and political control over holy lands led to conflict such as the Crusades.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the Umayyad and Abbasid caliphates, noting how the introduction of Islam changed the societies and cultures each conquered, blending with those societies and cultures and creating dynamic new Islamic societies and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Islam spread within the Mediterranean region from southwest Asia to northern Africa and the Iberian Peninsula.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how the Byzantine Empire preserved elements of the Roman Empire by blending Roman traditions with Greek culture, and developed a Christian faith, known as Orthodox Christianity, which united Church and state authority in the person of the emperor.

subject: Social Studies
description: The Byzantine Empire preserved elements of the Roman Empire, controlled lands within the Mediterranean basin, and began to develop Orthodox Christianity.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine reasons for the fall of the Roman Empire and the development of feudalism in Western Europe, including efforts to restore the empire, the decentralization of political authority, and the role of the Christian Church in providing some measure of central authority.

subject: Social Studies
description: Overexpansion, corruption, invasions, civil wars, and discord led to the fall of Rome. Feudalism developed in Western Europe in reaction to a need for order and to meet basic needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Mediterranean World: Feudal Western Europe, The Byzantine Empire, And The Islamic Caliphates (ca. 600 C.E. – ca. 1450): The Mediterranean world was reshaped with the fall of the Roman Empire. Three distinct cultural regions developed: feudal Western Europe, the Byzantine Empire, and the Islamic caliphates. These regions interacted with each other and clashed over control of holy lands.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine how cultural achievements of these civilizations have influenced contemporary societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine evidence related to the Qin, Han, and Greco-Roman (Athens and Roman Empire) civilizations and determine if these civilizations have experienced a golden age.

subject: Social Studies
description: A period of peace, prosperity, and cultural achievements may be indicative of a golden age.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the similarities and differences between the political systems of Chinese (Qin, Han) and Greco-Roman (Athens, Sparta, Roman Republic, Roman Empire) classical civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Political structures were developed to establish order, to create and enforce laws, and to enable decision making.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will compare and contrast the similarities and differences between the Chinese (Qin, Han) and Greco-Roman classical civilizations by examining religion, job specialization, cities, government, language/record keeping system, technology, and social hierarchy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will locate the classical civilizations on a map and identify geographic factors that influenced the extent of their boundaries, locate their cities on a map, and identify their political structures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Geographic factors influence the development of classical civilizations and their political structures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Comparative Classical Civilizations In The Eastern Hemisphere (ca. 600 B.C.E. – ca. 500 C.E.): As complex societies and civilizations change over time, their political and economic structures evolve. A golden age may be indicated when there is an extended period of time that is peaceful, prosperous, and demonstrates great cultural achievements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore the influence of various belief systems on contemporary cultures and events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will be able to identify similarities and differences across belief systems, including their effect on social order and gender roles.

subject: Social Studies
description: Belief systems and religions often are used to unify groups of people, and may affect social order and gender roles.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will study the belief systems of Judaism, Christianity, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, and Confucianism by looking at where the belief system originated, when it originated, founder(s) if any, and the major tenets, practices, and sacred writings or holy texts for each. (Note: Although not within this historic period, students may also study Sikhism and other major belief systems at this point.)

subject: Social Studies
description: Belief systems and religions are based on sets of mutually held values.

subject: Social Studies
description: Civilizations and complex societies developed belief systems and religions that have similar, as well as different, characteristics.

subject: Social Studies
description: Comparative World Religions (ca. 2000 B.C.E – ca. 630 C.E): Major religions and belief systems developed in the Eastern Hemisphere. There were important similarities and differences between these belief systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine the unique achievements of each of the selected complex societies and civilizations that served as lasting contributions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will compare and contrast the gender roles, access to wealth and power, and division of labor within the political and social structures of the selected river valley societies and civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Political and social hierarchies influenced the access that groups and individuals had to power, wealth, and jobs and influenced their roles within a society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore how the selected complex societies and civilizations adapted to and modified their environment to meet their basic needs of food, clothing, and shelter.

subject: Social Studies
description: Mesopotamia, Yellow River valley, Indus River valley, and Nile River valley complex societies and civilizations adapted to and modified their environment to meet the needs of their population.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore at least two river valley societies and civilizations: one in the Middle East (Mesopotamia or Nile river valley), one in South Asia (Indus River valley), or one in East Asia (Yellow River valley) by examining archaeological and historical evidence to compare and contrast characteristics of these complex societies and civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Complex societies and civilizations share the common characteristics of religion, job specialization, cities, government, language/record keeping system, technology, and social hierarchy. People in Mesopotamia, the Yellow River valley, the Indus River valley, and the Nile River valley developed complex societies and civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Humans living together in settlements develop shared customs, beliefs, ideas, and languages that give identity to the group.

subject: Social Studies
description: Early River Valley Civilizations In The Eastern Hemisphere (ca. 3500 B.C.E. – ca. 500 B.C.E.): Complex societies and civilizations developed in the Eastern Hemisphere. Although these complex societies and civilizations have certain defining characteristics in common, each is also known for unique cultural achievements and contributions. Early human communities in the Eastern Hemisphere adapted to and modified the physical environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will determine if the Neolithic Revolution is a turning point in world history, using various forms of evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Historians use archaeological and other types of evidence to investigate patterns in history and identify turning points. A turning point can be an event, era, and/or development in history that has brought about significant social, cultural, ecological, political, or economic change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will compare the use of tools and animals, types of dwellings, art, and social organizations of early peoples, and distinguish between the Paleolithic Age and Neolithic Age.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will be introduced to pastoral nomadic peoples as a culture type that existed throughout history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will explore early human migration patterns and settlements through the use of multiple maps and the examination of various forms of archaeological evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: The Neolithic Revolution was marked by technological advances in agriculture and domestication of animals that allowed people to form semi-sedentary and sedentary settlements.

subject: Social Studies
description: Early peoples in the Eastern Hemisphere are often studied by analyzing artifacts and archaeological features. Archaeologists engage in digs and study artifacts and features in a particular location to gather evidence about a group of people and how they lived at a particular time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Human populations that settled along rivers, in rainforests, along coastlines, in deserts, and in mountains made use of the resources and the environment around them in developing distinct ways of life.

subject: Social Studies
description: The First Humans Through The Neolithic Revolution In The Eastern Hemisphere: The first humans modified their physical environment as well as adapted to their environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will examine current political and environmental issues in a region or country of the Eastern Hemisphere being studied.

subject: Social Studies
description: Issues and problems experienced in the regions of the Eastern Hemisphere have roots in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: To understand scale, students will work with maps at a variety of scales so they can compare patterns in population density and land use, economic activity, and political connections across the present-day Eastern Hemisphere, within a region of the Eastern Hemisphere, and in a specific country. In doing so, students will examine maps of the hemisphere, three regions within the present-day Eastern Hemisphere, and one specific country within each region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Students will use physical, climate, and vegetation maps in combination with population density, land use, and resource distribution maps in order to discern patterns in human settlement, economic activity, and the relationship to scarcity of resources in the present-day Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: The physical environment influences human population distribution, land use, economic activities, and political connections.

subject: Social Studies
description: The Eastern Hemisphere can be divided into regions. Regions are areas that share common identifiable characteristics, such as physical, political, economic, or cultural features. Regions within the Eastern Hemisphere include:Middle East (North Africa and Southwest Asia), Sub-Saharan Africa, Europe (West, North, South, Central, and Southeast), Russia and the Independent States (Russia, Caucasia, Central Asia, the region of Belarus, Moldova, and Ukraine), East Asia (People's Republic of China, North Korea, South Korea, Japan, and Taiwan), Southeast Asia (Vietnam, Cambodia, Laos, Thailand, Myanmar [Burma], Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Brunei, Philippines), South Asia (Afghanistan, Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan), and Oceania (Australia, New Zealand, the Pacific)

subject: Social Studies
description: Maps can be used to represent varied climate zones, landforms, bodies of water, and resources of the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Present-Day Eastern Hemisphere Geography: The diverse geography of the Eastern Hemisphere has influenced human culture and settlement patterns in distinct ways. Human communities in the Eastern Hemisphere have adapted to or modified the physical environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: 9. Develop an understanding of an interdependent global community by developing awareness and/or engaging in the political process as it relates to a global context.

subject: Social Studies
description: 8. Identify rights and responsibilities of citizens within societies in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: 7. Describe the roles of people in power in the Eastern Hemisphere both historically and currently. Identify ways that current figures can influence people’s rights and freedom.

subject: Social Studies
description: 6. Identify situations with a global focus in which social actions are required and suggest solutions.

subject: Social Studies
description: 5. Participate in negotiating and compromising in the resolution of differences and conflict; introduce and examine the role of conflict resolution.

subject: Social Studies
description: 4. Identify and describe opportunities for and the role of the individual in social and political participation at various times and in various locations in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: 3. Identify and explore different types of political systems and ideologies used at various times and in various locations in the Eastern Hemisphere and identify the role of individuals and key groups in those political and social systems.

subject: Social Studies
description: 2. Participate in activities that focus on a local issue or problem in a country in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: 1. Demonstrate respect for the rights of others in discussion and classroom debates, regardless of whether one agrees with the other viewpoint. Consider alternate views in discussion.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe government decisions that affect economies in case studies from the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Provide examples of unemployment, inflation, total production, income, and economic growth in economies in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the role of job specialization and trade historically and during contemporary times in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare market economies to other economic systems in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the role that various types of resources (human capital, physical capital, and natural resources) have in providing goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how scarcity necessitates decision making; employ examples from the Eastern Hemisphere to illustrate the role of scarcity historically and in current events; compare through historical examples the costs and benefits of economic decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the spatial organization of place, considering the historical, social, political, and economic implication of that organization. Recognize that boundaries and definitions of location are historically constructed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how human activities alter places and regions in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and explain how characteristics (cultural, economic, and physical-environmental) of regions affect the history of societies in the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe how environments affect human activities and how human activities affect physical environments through the study of cases in the Eastern Hemisphere. Grades K-8 Page 80

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish human activities and human-made features from “environments” (natural events or physical features—land, air, and water—that are not directly made by humans) in the Eastern Hemisphere; identify the relationship between human activities and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Use location terms and geographic representations such as maps, photographs, satellite images, and models to describe where places in the Eastern Hemisphere are in relation to each other, to describe connections between places, and to evaluate the benefits of particular places for purposeful activities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the roles that periodization and region play in developing the comparison of historical civilizations. Identify general characteristics that can be employed to conduct comparative analysis of case studies in the Eastern Hemisphere in the same historical period, with teacher support.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe historical developments in the history of the Eastern Hemisphere, with specific references to circumstances of time and place and to connections to broader regional or global processes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify how the relationship between geography, economics, and history helps to define a context for events in the study of the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe and compare multiple events in the history of the Eastern Hemisphere in societies in similar chronological contexts and in various geographical contexts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Categorize and evaluate divergent perspectives on an individual historical event.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify a region in the Eastern Hemisphere by describing a characteristic that places within it have in common, and then compare it to other regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand that historians use periodization to categorize events. Describe general models of periodization in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the relationships of patterns of continuity and change to larger historical processes and themes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare histories in different places in the Eastern Hemisphere, utilizing time lines. Identify ways that changing periodization affects the historical narrative.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize and analyze the dynamics of historical continuity and change over periods of time. Identify the role of turning points as an important dynamic in historical change.

subject: Social Studies
description: Distinguish between long-term and immediate causes and effects of an event from current events or history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and classify the relationship between multiple causes and multiple effects.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify causes and effects from current events, grade-level content, and historical events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Employ mathematical skills to measure time by years, decades, centuries, and millennia; to calculate time from the fixed points of the calendar system (B.C.E. and C.E.); and to interpret the data presented in time lines, with teacher support.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways that events are related chronologically to one another in time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize arguments on specific social studies topics and identify evidence to support the arguments. Examine arguments related to a specific social studies topic from multiple perspectives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify implicit ideas and draw inferences, with support.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the arguments of others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify evidence and explain content, authorship, point of view, purpose, and format; identify bias; explain the role of bias and potential audience.

subject: Social Studies
